label: Al-Andalus
pitch:
For nearly eight centuries, Islamic Iberia produced the Caliphate of Córdoba — for a moment the most sophisticated state in western Europe — the philosopher who taught Christendom how to read Aristotle, the largest library west of Baghdad, and the Alhambra. It is also the civilization the modern world cannot stop arguing about: whether convivencia, the living-together of Muslims, Christians, and Jews, was a tolerant golden age or a hierarchy enforced by holy war.

🔑 Key Facts:
• Abd al-Rahman I, the lone Umayyad survivor of the Abbasid massacre, founded an independent emirate at Córdoba in 756
• Abd al-Rahman III declared the Caliphate of Córdoba in 929 and built the palace-city of Madinat al-Zahra
• Averroes (Ibn Rushd) and Maimonides, born in the same city a dozen years apart, reshaped European and Jewish thought
• The Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa in 1212 broke Almohad power and triggered the Great Reconquista

🤔 Why It Matters: The Toledo translators turned captured Arabic manuscripts into the Latin raw material of the European intellectual revival, the medieval universities, and ultimately the Scientific Revolution. Al-Andalus was the loading dock through which the ancient world re-entered the West.

🎲 Wild Card: The holiest spot in the Great Mosque of Córdoba was decorated with gold mosaic by Greek Orthodox craftsmen sent from Constantinople by the Byzantine emperor — a Christian gift to the chief mosque of his Muslim rival.

spans.label: Conquest & Province
spans.description: A Berber-and-Arab army under Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 711 and, within roughly five years, overran the Visigothic kingdom. Al-Andalus was governed as a chronically unstable far-western province of the Umayyad Caliphate, torn by Arab–Berber feuding.

spans.label: Emirate of Córdoba
spans.description: Abd al-Rahman I, the surviving Umayyad prince, founded an independent emirate at Córdoba. The state survived muwallad and Mozarab revolts, the Córdoba martyrs, and the long Ibn Hafsun rebellion, while the population slowly converted to Islam.

spans.label: Caliphate of Córdoba
spans.description: Abd al-Rahman III proclaimed the Caliphate in 929, making Córdoba the most powerful state in the western Mediterranean. After the dictatorship of Almanzor, the Caliphate destroyed itself in the Fitna and was abolished in 1031.

spans.label: Taifas & Almoravids
spans.description: The Caliphate fractured into roughly thirty rival taifa city-states — politically a catastrophe, culturally a golden age. Paying tribute to Christian kings, the taifas summoned the Saharan Almoravids, who conquered them and annexed al-Andalus to a Berber empire ruled from Marrakesh.

spans.label: Almohad Caliphate
spans.description: The Almohads, a fundamentalist Berber movement founded by the self-declared Mahdi Ibn Tumart, replaced the Almoravids. Their century was both the most religiously intolerant of the story and the century of Averroes, Ibn Tufayl, and Ibn Arabi, until Las Navas de Tolosa shattered their power in 1212.

spans.label: Emirate of Granada
spans.description: The Nasrid Emirate of Granada, a tribute-paying vassal of Castile, survived for two and a half centuries by diplomacy, side-switching, and the Alhambra's mountain defenses, until the union of Castile and Aragon finished the Granada War in 1492.

spans.label: Moriscos & Expulsion
spans.description: The Capitulations of Granada were overridden within a decade; forced conversion produced the Moriscos, who after a century of suspicion and revolt were expelled from Spain entirely between 1609 and 1614 — the literal end of al-Andalus.

events.label: The Strait of Gibraltar
events.description: The eight-mile channel separating Iberia from North Africa, the one place the Islamic world and Latin Christendom shared a crossable land frontier for eight centuries.

events.label: The Visigothic Kingdom
events.description: The Germanic warrior aristocracy that ruled Hispania from Toledo over a far larger Hispano-Roman Christian and Jewish population, chronically destabilized by elective-throne civil wars.

events.label: The Umayyad Caliphate
events.description: The Damascus-based dynasty whose armies, by 700, had conquered an arc from the borders of India to the Atlantic coast of Morocco, the fastest imperial expansion then on record.

events.label: Islamization of the Berbers
events.description: The indigenous peoples of North Africa, conquered and largely converted, who supplied the bulk of the armies on the Maghreb frontier — most of the 711 invaders were recently Islamized Berbers, not Arabs.

events.label: The Convivencia Debate
events.description: The still-unresolved argument — Castro versus Sánchez-Albornoz, Menocal versus Fernández-Morera — over whether al-Andalus was a tolerant multi-faith civilization or a hierarchy of holy war.

events.label: Tariq ibn Ziyad Crosses to Iberia
events.description: The Berber freedman Tariq landed an army of perhaps 7,000 near the rock that bears his name, Jabal Tariq — Gibraltar — opening the conquest of Spain.

events.label: Battle of Guadalete
events.description: King Roderic's Visigothic army disintegrated in a battle near the Guadalete river; Roderic vanished from history and the kingdom collapsed with him.

events.label: Musa ibn Nusayr's Campaign
events.description: The Arab governor of the Maghreb crossed in 712 with a second, more heavily Arab army to claim the conquest personally; between him and Tariq the peninsula fell in about five years.

events.label: Fall of Toledo and the Visigothic State
events.description: Toledo, Córdoba, Seville, and Zaragoza fell in rapid succession; for most inhabitants the conquest was experienced less as catastrophe than as a change of management.

events.label: Treaty of Tudmir
events.description: A surviving surrender pact between the Arab command and the Visigothic lord Theodemir, guaranteeing churches and worship in exchange for tribute — the clearest evidence of how the conquest actually worked.

events.label: The Dhimma Established
events.description: The legal contract under which conquered Christians and Jews kept their faith, law, and property as protected but subordinate subjects in exchange for the jizya poll tax.

events.label: Battle of Tours
events.description: Charles Martel's Franks met and turned back a raiding army under al-Ghafiqi; later mythologized by Gibbon as the battle that 'saved Christendom,' though modern historians see a large raid, not an invasion.

events.label: Battle of Covadonga
events.description: Pelagius's small victory in the Asturian mountains, militarily a skirmish, mythologized as the seed of the 770-year Reconquista and the Kingdom of Asturias.

events.label: Kingdom of Asturias
events.description: The small Christian kingdom that grew from Pelagius's resistance in the unconquered wet green north, the acorn from which every later Reconquista claim grew.

events.label: Umayyad Conquest of Sind
events.description: In the same year as Tariq's crossing, the Umayyad Caliphate was conquering Sind on the Indus — the empire was punching into two continents at once.

events.label: The Great Berber Revolt
events.description: A massive uprising of Berber soldiers, resentful at being given the worst land while Arabs took the southern river valleys, spread from Morocco into Spain and reshaped the province.

events.label: Abbasid Revolution
events.description: The Umayyad dynasty was overthrown and massacred by the Abbasids, who moved the Caliphate's capital to Iraq — the event that split the histories of the Islamic east and the Islamic west.

events.label: Abd al-Rahman I
events.description: The lone Umayyad survivor of the Abbasid massacre, who fled the width of the Islamic world and made himself ruler of al-Andalus — 'the Falcon of the Quraysh.'

events.label: Emirate of Córdoba Founded
events.description: Abd al-Rahman I declared an independent emirate, still nominally acknowledging the Abbasid caliph — the first time the Islamic far west was a state, not a province.

events.label: Great Mosque of Córdoba Begun
events.description: Abd al-Rahman I began the Mezquita on the site of a church over a Roman temple, modeled on the lost Umayyad mosque of Damascus — building Syria in Spain.

events.label: Charlemagne's Spanish Campaign
events.description: Invited by rebel Muslim governors of the Ebro valley, the Frankish king reached Zaragoza, failed to take it, and turned back.

events.label: Ambush at Roncesvalles
events.description: Charlemagne's rearguard was annihilated in a Pyrenean pass by Christian Basques, an event rewritten three centuries later as the Song of Roland, a holy war the ambush never was.

events.label: The Song of Roland
events.description: The founding epic of French literature, turning the 778 Basque ambush into Roland's glorious death against hundreds of thousands of Saracens — the classic case of needed history outrunning real history.

events.label: The Marca Hispanica
events.description: The Frankish frontier province organized after the capture of Barcelona in 801, the seed of Catalonia and eventually the Crown of Aragon that would finish the story.

events.label: Abd al-Rahman II
events.description: The emir whose Córdoba court drew the musician and tastemaker Ziryab and absorbed eastern fashions, music, and cuisine.

events.label: Ziryab at Córdoba
events.description: The musician and arbiter of taste credited — in much-embroidered tradition — with a fifth oud string, a music conservatory, and the restructuring of Córdoban fashion and dining.

events.label: Viking Sack of Seville
events.description: Scandinavian raiders struck the Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts of al-Andalus, sacking Seville in 844 — the Viking phenomenon seen from the receiving end.

events.label: The Martyrs of Córdoba
events.description: Around four dozen Christians sought execution between 850 and 859 by publicly denouncing Muhammad — a protest against assimilation, not against persecution.

events.label: Eulogius of Córdoba
events.description: The Córdoban priest who chronicled, encouraged, and finally joined the martyr movement, executed in 859 — the best witness and least neutral source for it.

events.label: Paul Alvarus's Lament
events.description: The Córdoban Christian writer's bitter complaint that his fellow Christians could no longer write a Latin letter but composed polished Arabic verse — assimilation as despair.

events.label: Umar ibn Hafsun's Revolt
events.description: The muwallad rebel who fortified the mountain stronghold of Bobastro and defied the Emirate for forty years, nearly bringing the state down.

events.label: The Fortress of Bobastro
events.description: Ibn Hafsun's almost impregnable mountain capital south of Córdoba, the base of a forty-year rebellion finally taken in 928.

events.label: The Banu Qasi Dynasty
events.description: A muwallad clan on the northern frontier, descended from a converted Visigothic count, who ran their own border principality playing all sides for generations.

events.label: The Conversion S-Curve
events.description: Richard Bulliet's model suggesting Muslims became a majority in al-Andalus only around the year 1000 — for its first three centuries 'Islamic Spain' was mostly non-Muslim.

events.label: The Mozarabs
events.description: Christians under Muslim rule who kept their faith and Latin liturgy but lived in Arabic, becoming a distinct culture that was neither the Visigothic past nor the Muslim present.

events.label: Abd al-Rahman III
events.description: Acceded at twenty-one to a state that barely ran Córdoba and, over a 49-year reign, made it the most powerful in western Europe — the closest thing al-Andalus has to a colossus.

events.label: Fall of Bobastro
events.description: Abd al-Rahman III finally crushed the Ibn Hafsun resistance and took Bobastro, exhuming and crucifying the long-dead rebel's corpse to prove the war was over.

events.label: Caliphate of Córdoba Declared
events.description: Abd al-Rahman III proclaimed himself Caliph, a counter to the Fatimid claim across the strait — the Mediterranean now had three rival caliphs at once.

events.label: The Fatimid Challenge
events.description: The Shia Fatimid dynasty declared its own caliphate in North Africa and pushed toward Morocco, the strategic backyard of al-Andalus — the trigger for Córdoba's caliphal claim.

events.label: Madinat al-Zahra Built
events.description: Abd al-Rahman III's purpose-built palace-city outside Córdoba, terraced in three levels of escalating splendor to make caliphal power legible to any visitor.

events.label: The Córdoban Gold Dinar
events.description: A gold coin of dependable full weight, trusted across the western Mediterranean — a reliable sign that the Caliphate's revenues and credibility were both high.

events.label: Hasdai ibn Shaprut
events.description: The Jewish physician who became Abd al-Rahman III's diplomat and effective foreign minister, patron of a renaissance of Hebrew poetry and learning in Spain.

events.label: Embassy of John of Gorze
events.description: The monk-envoy of Otto I kept waiting in Córdoba for some three years because his letter insulted Islam — a well-attested glimpse of the Caliphate's bureaucratic patience.

events.label: The Saqaliba
events.description: European slave-soldiers and slave-officials, many from the Slavic and Frankish lands, built into a force loyal to the dynasty rather than to Arab clans — a system that would later turn dangerous.

events.label: Al-Hakam II
events.description: The bookish caliph who built the great library, founded twenty-seven free schools for Córdoba's poor, and completed the most beautiful phase of the Great Mosque.

events.label: The Library of Córdoba
events.description: The caliphal library, reputedly hundreds of thousands of volumes, in the league of Baghdad and Constantinople when great Christian collections held a few hundred manuscripts.

events.label: The Mihrab Mosaics
events.description: Al-Hakam II's new mihrab and dome, sheathed in gold mosaic by Greek Orthodox craftsmen sent from Constantinople by the Byzantine emperor — a Christian gift to a Muslim rival's mosque.

events.label: The Byzantine Dioscorides Gift
events.description: A deluxe Greek copy of Dioscorides' herbal sent by Constantinople as an instrument of statecraft, the spark of Córdoba's medical translation effort.

events.label: Hisham II
events.description: The eleven-year-old who inherited the Caliphate, kept as a sacred figurehead while Almanzor ruled in his name.

events.label: Almanzor (al-Mansur)
events.description: The non-royal chamberlain who made himself absolute ruler, took al-Andalus to its military peak, and built the army whose disloyalty destroyed the state.

events.label: Madinat al-Zahira
events.description: Almanzor's rival palace-city, built to drain power away from the caliphal capital and run al-Andalus as a military dictatorship.

events.label: Sack of Santiago de Compostela
events.description: Almanzor sacked Christian Spain's holiest shrine and had the cathedral bells carried to Córdoba on captives' shoulders — the most symbolically loaded act of the frontier war.

events.label: The Razzias of Almanzor
events.description: Some fifty near-annual raids into Christian territory, almost none lost — Barcelona in 985, León, Santiago in 997 — that made the north look finished around the year 1000.

events.label: Death of Almanzor
events.description: Almanzor died returning from a raid; his able son held the dictatorship to 1008, his incompetent second son detonated it in 1009.

events.label: The Fitna of al-Andalus
events.description: A twenty-two-year multi-sided civil war among the factions Almanzor had armed; nine or more caliphs proclaimed and murdered, Madinat al-Zahra burned.

events.label: Sack of Madinat al-Zahra
events.description: Abd al-Rahman III's shining city was sacked and burned less than eighty years after it was built — why it is an excavation today and not a standing palace.

events.label: The Caliphate Abolished
events.description: With no credible caliph left, Córdoba's leading citizens simply ended the office — the most powerful state in the western Mediterranean had killed itself.

events.label: The Taifa Kingdoms
events.description: Roughly thirty independent Muslim city-states splintered from the Caliphate — politically a catastrophe, culturally perhaps the most brilliant period al-Andalus ever had.

events.label: Abbadid Seville
events.description: The taifa dynasty of judges-turned-kings that made Seville the leading court of the era, a permanent literary salon under its poet-king al-Mu'tamid.

events.label: Al-Mu'tamid of Seville
events.description: The poet-king of Seville who chose to summon the Almoravids rather than submit to Castile, and died a prisoner in a Moroccan desert oasis writing exile poetry.

events.label: Ibn Hazm
events.description: The literalist Córdoban jurist and theologian who also wrote The Ring of the Dove, the tenderest prose anatomy of love in Arabic — al-Andalus's signature paradox in one man.

events.label: The Ring of the Dove
events.description: Ibn Hazm's strikingly intimate treatise on the psychology of love, jealousy, and longing, still read and startlingly modern.

events.label: The Parias Tribute
events.description: Protection-money payments in gold from terrified taifa kings to Christian rulers — the mechanism by which Andalusi wealth financed its own conquest.

events.label: Samuel ibn Naghrillah
events.description: The practicing Jew who served as vizier and army commander of the taifa kingdom of Granada while being one of the great Hebrew poets of the age.

events.label: The 1066 Granada Massacre
events.description: The Muslim population of Granada rose and massacred the Jewish community, killing the vizier Joseph ibn Naghrillah — the single fact that most cleanly refutes the utopia reading.

events.label: Alfonso VI Takes Toledo
events.description: The permanent fall of the old Visigothic capital to Castile, a capital-scale shock, and the working multi-faith city whose libraries became the great translation factory.

events.label: Alfonso VI of León-Castile
events.description: The Christian king who turned the parias protection racket into statecraft and took Toledo in 1085, ending the taifa idyll.

events.label: Solomon ibn Gabirol
events.description: One of the great Hebrew poets and a Neoplatonic philosopher of the Jewish Golden Age in Spain, known in Latin Europe as Avicebron.

events.label: Judah Halevi
events.description: The greatest Hebrew poet of the age and author of the Kuzari, who left Spain at the end of his life for the Holy Land.

events.label: The Almoravids
events.description: A movement of veiled Sanhaja Berber desert puritans from the western Sahara, summoned by the taifas and ruling from Marrakesh.

events.label: Yusuf ibn Tashfin
events.description: The Almoravid commander who conquered Morocco, founded Marrakesh, won at Zallaqa, then deposed the taifa kings who had invited him.

events.label: Marrakesh Founded
events.description: The Almoravid capital in Morocco, the seat from which a Berber empire would rule al-Andalus as a province.

events.label: Battle of Sagrajas (Zallaqa)
events.description: Yusuf ibn Tashfin crushed Alfonso VI near Badajoz, checking the Christian advance and, for a moment, seeming to save al-Andalus.

events.label: Almoravid Annexation of the Taifas
events.description: Between 1090 and 1094 the Almoravids conquered the taifa kingdoms — not the Christians — deposing the very kings who had called them.

events.label: El Cid Takes Valencia
events.description: Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar, a mercenary captain who fought for and against both faiths, carved out a personal principality by conquering Valencia, ruling it until his death in 1099.

events.label: Battle of Manzikert
events.description: The Seljuk Turks shattered the Byzantine army in eastern Anatolia, one prong of a civilization-wide moment of stress on the Islamic and Christian worlds in the same generation as Zallaqa.

events.label: The First Crusade
events.description: The Christian holy war launched to take Jerusalem; its armies massacred Rhineland Jewish communities en route, the same decade the Almoravids stormed up out of the Sahara.

events.label: Al-Ghazali and the Book-Burning
events.description: The Almoravids' rigid Maliki jurists are reported to have burned the works of al-Ghazali, the era's most influential theologian — a sign of brittle orthodoxy.

events.label: The Maliki Jurists
events.description: The rigid legal faction the Almoravids leaned on, one of the four schools of Sunni law, dominant in North Africa and al-Andalus.

events.label: Ibn Tumart
events.description: The Atlas Berber preacher who absorbed al-Ghazali's theology, declared himself the Mahdi, and founded the Almohad reform movement around the absolute oneness of God.

events.label: The Almohad Movement
events.description: The al-Muwahhidun, 'those who affirm the oneness,' a militant church with an infallible Mahdi founder, hostile to the Maliki tradition.

events.label: Abd al-Mu'min
events.description: Ibn Tumart's successor, a commander of genius who destroyed the Almoravids, took Marrakesh in 1147, and built the largest single empire the Islamic west ever produced.

events.label: Almohad Conquest of al-Andalus
events.description: The Almohads crossed into Spain and replaced the Almoravids, ruling North Africa from the Atlantic to Egypt and the southern half of Iberia from Marrakesh.

events.label: The Almohad Persecution
events.description: The Almohads largely abolished the dhimma, giving Jews and Christians conversion, exile, or death — shattering communities that had survived four centuries.

events.label: The Giralda Built
events.description: The minaret of the Almohad great mosque of Seville, the austere geometric aesthetic of the 'unitarian' reformers turned into a skyline, now a cathedral bell-tower.

events.label: The Torre del Oro
events.description: The Almohad river-tower guarding the Guadalquivir at Seville, one of the regime's signature surviving structures.

events.label: Battle of Alarcos
events.description: The Almohad caliph Abu Yusuf Yaqub al-Mansur crushed Castile, stalling the Christian advance for a few years — the empire's brief high-water mark.

events.label: Averroes (Ibn Rushd)
events.description: The Córdoban judge and physician whose close commentaries on Aristotle, translated at Toledo, reorganized European thought — to the Latin schoolmen simply 'the Commentator.'

events.label: Ibn Tufayl
events.description: The Almohad-era philosopher who wrote Hayy ibn Yaqzan, a desert-island novel about whether unaided reason can reach truth, five and a half centuries before Robinson Crusoe.

events.label: Hayy ibn Yaqzan
events.description: Ibn Tufayl's philosophical novel of a child raised alone on an island reasoning his way to knowledge of nature and God; a 1671 Latin translation circulated in England.

events.label: Ibn Bajja (Avempace)
events.description: The earlier philosopher and polymath of Zaragoza and the Maghreb who carried the Andalusi philosophical tradition into the twelfth century.

events.label: Maimonides
events.description: Moses ben Maimon, born in Córdoba, driven out by Almohad persecution, who became physician at Saladin's court and the most important Jewish thinker of the medieval era.

events.label: The Guide for the Perplexed
events.description: Maimonides' Arabic reconciliation of revealed scripture with Aristotelian philosophy, the Jewish parallel to Averroes and Aquinas, an influence on Aquinas directly.

events.label: Ibn Arabi
events.description: The Murcia-born 'Greatest Master,' the most influential mystical metaphysician in the history of Islam, who carried Andalusi thought east into Sufism for seven centuries.

events.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa
events.description: The combined armies of Castile, Aragon, and Navarre destroyed the Almohad field army in an afternoon — the single most decisive battle in the entire eight-century story.

events.label: The Sack of Constantinople (1204)
events.description: Eight years before Las Navas, a Catholic crusader army sacked Christian Constantinople — the cross triumphant on one Mediterranean front and self-wounded on the other.

events.label: The Great Reconquista
events.description: With no Almohad army left, the great cities of al-Andalus fell to the Christian kingdoms within a single thirty-year generation.

events.label: Córdoba Falls to Castile
events.description: Ferdinand III took the old caliphal capital; the Great Mosque was reconsecrated as a cathedral and, by tradition, Almanzor's bells were carried back north.

events.label: Valencia Falls to Aragon
events.description: James I of Aragon took Valencia, part of the avalanche that reduced al-Andalus to a single mountain kingdom in a generation.

events.label: Seville Falls to Castile
events.description: Ferdinand III took the largest city in al-Andalus and the Almohad capital in Spain after a long siege.

events.label: The Mudéjars
events.description: Muslims under Christian rule after the great conquests, granted a deal structurally identical to the dhimma — convivencia running in both directions under both faiths.

events.label: The Toledo School of Translators
events.description: Under Toledo's archbishops, a sustained enterprise rendered captured Arabic science and philosophy into Latin — the loading dock through which the ancient world re-entered the West.

events.label: Gerard of Cremona
events.description: The Italian who moved to Toledo because that was where the books were and personally translated some seventy works, including Ptolemy's Almagest.

events.label: Al-Zarqali (Arzachel)
events.description: The Toledo astronomer who compiled the Toledan Tables, the most accurate astronomical tables in the West for two centuries, cited into Copernicus's time.

events.label: The Toledan Tables
events.description: Precomputed astronomical charts from al-Zarqali's Toledo school, the indispensable Western reference for calendars, navigation, and astrology for two hundred years.

events.label: Al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis)
events.description: The Córdoban whose thirty-volume medical encyclopedia, its illustrated surgical volume, remained the standard surgical textbook in Europe for roughly six hundred years.

events.label: Ibn Zuhr (Avenzoar)
events.description: The Seville physician who advanced clinical description and experimental method in Andalusi medicine.

events.label: Ibn al-Baytar
events.description: The botanist from Málaga who catalogued over a thousand plants and drugs, a pharmacological reference for centuries.

events.label: The Arab Agricultural Revolution
events.description: A debated label for a real cluster of new crops — rice, citrus, sugar, cotton, eggplant — and irrigation engineering that entered Europe through Spain.

events.label: The Acequia Irrigation Networks
events.description: Gravity-fed channel systems, norias, and water-allocation institutions so durable the Tribunal de las Aguas of Valencia still meets weekly.

events.label: Tribunal de las Aguas
events.description: The Valencia water court that adjudicates irrigation disputes, often called one of the oldest continuously functioning judicial institutions in Europe.

events.label: Arabic Loanwords in Spanish
events.description: Roughly eight percent of the Spanish vocabulary is of Arabic origin, concentrated in water, farming, and building — the most pervasive everyday fossil of al-Andalus.

events.label: Aquinas and Latin Averroism
events.description: The thirteenth-century European confrontation with Aristotle: Aquinas's synthesis and the rival Latin Averroist current the bishop of Paris condemned in 1277.

events.label: The Muwashshah and the Kharja
events.description: The Andalusi strophic song-poem whose closing kharja, often a woman's voice in early Romance, is among the oldest surviving lyric poetry in any Romance language.

events.label: The Horseshoe Arch
events.description: A Visigothic form the conquerors adopted and made their own, evidence in stone that al-Andalus synthesized on the spot rather than importing wholesale from Damascus.

events.label: The Andalusi Courtyard Garden
events.description: The walled green order with water at its center, built as a deliberate image of the Quranic paradise — direct ancestor of the Spanish patio.

events.label: The Troubadour Question
events.description: The unresolved argument over whether the Provençal troubadour lyric of courtly love was influenced by Andalusi song — suggestive adjacency, thin hard evidence.

events.label: Emirate of Granada Founded
events.description: Muhammad I ibn al-Ahmar founded the Nasrid emirate, the last piece of al-Andalus, which would survive 254 years.

events.label: Muhammad I ibn al-Ahmar
events.description: Founder of the Nasrid dynasty, who became Ferdinand III's vassal in 1246 and helped Castile take Muslim Seville to buy his kingdom's survival.

events.label: Granada's Vassalage to Castile
events.description: The founding bargain: enormous annual tribute, troops on demand, and acknowledged Castilian overlordship in exchange for survival.

events.label: The Alhambra
events.description: The Nasrid palace-citadel above Granada, the best-preserved medieval Islamic palace complex, its effect achieved entirely through surface, water, and inscribed poetry.

events.label: The Court of the Lions
events.description: The Alhambra court whose Ibn Zamrak verses, carved beside the fountain they describe, make the building narrate itself to the person standing in it.

events.label: Ibn al-Khatib
events.description: Nasrid Granada's greatest scholar, vizier and physician, who argued from observation that the Black Death spread by contagion, and was strangled in a Moroccan prison.

events.label: Ibn Zamrak
events.description: The Nasrid court poet whose verses are carved on the walls of the Alhambra, the architecture inscribed with its own description.

events.label: The Marinid Counterweight
events.description: The Berber dynasty that overthrew the Almohads in Morocco, played against Castile by the Nasrids as a North African counterweight.

events.label: Ibn Battuta Visits Granada
events.description: The most widely traveled person of the medieval world recorded a Nasrid kingdom still confident and brilliant, with a century and a half left to run.

events.label: The Granada Silk Trade
events.description: Granada's famous luxury export, woven from local mulberry-fed silkworms and prized across Christian Europe, sustaining the last great Andalusi entrepôt.

events.label: The Catholic Monarchs
events.description: The 1469 marriage of Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon united the two largest Christian crowns into a single coordinated power for the first time.

events.label: The Spanish Inquisition
events.description: Established to investigate conversos suspected of secretly practicing Judaism, it helped finance the Granada War and shaped the chapter's ending.

events.label: The Granada War
events.description: A methodical ten-year town-by-town reduction with siege artillery the Nasrids could not match, helped by a three-way Nasrid civil war.

events.label: Boabdil (Muhammad XII)
events.description: The last Nasrid emir, played against his own father and uncle by the Catholic Monarchs, who handed over the Alhambra in 1492.

events.label: The Fall of Málaga
events.description: After a long siege the city's surviving population was killed or enslaved almost entirely, a deliberate demonstration of what resistance would cost.

events.label: The Fall of Granada
events.description: On January 2, 1492, Boabdil surrendered the keys of the Alhambra — 801 years after Tariq landed, no Muslim state remained in western Europe.

events.label: The Capitulations of Granada
events.description: The surrender treaty guaranteeing the Muslims of Granada their religion, law, property, and language — the dhimma logic of 711 running in reverse, void within a decade.

events.label: The Alhambra Decree
events.description: The March 1492 expulsion of all Jews who would not convert, ending Europe's longest-rooted Jewish community and scattering the Sephardim.

events.label: The Sephardic Diaspora
events.description: The exiled Jews of Spain, carrying Ladino — medieval Spanish in Hebrew letters — across the Mediterranean, many welcomed by the Ottoman sultan Bayezid II.

events.label: Columbus Gets His Contract
events.description: From the Santa Fe siege camp the same triumphant Catholic state that finished the Reconquista in January funded Columbus in spring; he sailed in August.

events.label: Cardinal Cisneros
events.description: The most powerful churchman in Spain, who from 1499 pushed mass forced conversion and Quran burnings, overriding the Capitulations.

events.label: Forced Conversion of the Muslims
events.description: After a revolt the crown declared the Capitulations void; in 1502 the Muslims of Castile were ordered to convert or leave, extended across Spain thereafter.

events.label: The Moriscos
events.description: The converted Muslims and their descendants — Christian by law, suspected of secret Islam in fact — Spain's internal scapegoat for a century.

events.label: War of the Alpujarras
events.description: The brutal Morisco revolt in the mountain valleys outside Granada, crushed in 1571 and followed by the forced dispersal of the region's Moriscos across Castile.

events.label: Expulsion of the Moriscos
events.description: The 1609–1614 deportation of an estimated 300,000 Spanish-born baptized Christians to a North Africa they had never seen — the literal end of al-Andalus.

events.label: The Mosque-Cathedral of Córdoba
events.description: A Gothic-Renaissance church dropped into the middle of the column forest in the 1520s; Charles V reportedly said they had destroyed something unique to build something ordinary.

events.label: Washington Irving's Tales of the Alhambra
events.description: The American writer's 1832 book that, building on earlier Romantic travelers, fixed the modern romantic image of Moorish Spain and launched the tourism that funds the Alhambra's survival.

events.label: Al-Maqqari's Anthology
events.description: The North African scholar who in the 1600s assembled the great anthology that is still the main narrative source for the whole period.

events.label: Ibn al-Qutiya
events.description: The tenth-century Córdoban historian, descended from a Visigothic princess, whose chronicle is a key early source for the conquest tradition.

events.label: The Reconquista
events.description: The 770-year project, with no original master plan, by which Christian kingdoms retook the peninsula — a unified holy crusade only in back-projection.
